message:
feat(wa): implement backup import command (wa-wb6)

Add wa backup import for loading data from backup archives:
- Verify-only mode, dry-run mode, safety backup of current DB
- Schema compatibility check, interactive confirmation
- JSON output for automation, WAL/journal cleanup
- 5 new tests (roundtrip, dry-run, safety, rejection, manifest)
- Total: 954/954 wa-core tests pass

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-3g9.3","title":"Connection pooling for WezTerm: reuse mux connections when available","description":"
# Connection Pooling for WezTerm

## Purpose
Reduce overhead of WezTerm CLI calls by pooling connections to the mux server.

## Current Behavior
Each WezTerm CLI call spawns a new process, connects to mux, executes, and disconnects.

## Proposed Behavior
When using vendored mode (direct mux protocol):
```rust
pub struct WezTermPool {
    connections: Vec<MuxConnection>,
    max_size: usize,
    idle_timeout: Duration,
}

impl WezTermPool {
    pub async fn execute<T>(&self, cmd: MuxCommand) -> Result<T> {
        let conn = self.acquire().await?;
        let result = conn.execute(cmd).await;
        self.release(conn);
        result
    }
}
```

## Benefits
- Avoid process spawn overhead (~10ms per call)
- Reduce socket churn
- Enable connection keepalive
- Batch multiple operations efficiently

## Prerequisites
- Requires vendored mode (wa-nu4.4.1)
- Direct mux protocol access

## Fallback
CLI mode: No pooling possible, but can batch commands.

## Benchmark Targets
- With pooling: ~1ms per operation
- Without pooling: ~10-20ms per operation
- 10x improvement for high-frequency operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Connection pool implemented for vendored mode
- [ ] Idle connection cleanup
- [ ] Graceful fallback for CLI mode
- [ ] Benchmark proves improvement

## Testing
- Criterion benchmarks with budgets and baseline comparison.
- Microbenchmarks for the specific optimization.
- CI regression guard with perf logs.

 {"id":"wa-3g9.4","title":"Incremental FTS sync: avoid full reindex, track per-pane progress","description":"
# Incremental FTS Sync

## Purpose
Ensure FTS index only processes new content, avoiding expensive full reindex operations.

## Current Challenge
On restart, wa might need to determine what's already indexed and what's new.

## Solution
```rust
pub struct FtsIndexState {
    /// Last indexed segment seq per pane
    per_pane_progress: HashMap<PaneId, SegmentSeq>,
    /// Global index version for schema changes
    index_version: u32,
}
```

## Sync Process
1. On startup: Load `per_pane_progress` from DB
2. For each pane: Query segments where `seq > last_indexed_seq`
3. Index only new segments
4. Update progress after successful batch

## Batching Strategy
- Batch size: 100 segments or 1MB text
- Commit progress after each batch
- Resume from last batch on crash

## Performance Impact
- Full reindex: O(total segments)
- Incremental: O(new segments)
- Startup overhead: O(1) - just load progress

## Recovery
If index corrupt or schema changed:
- Detect via `index_version` mismatch
- Trigger full rebuild
- Show progress indicator

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Per-pane progress tracked in DB
- [ ] Only new segments indexed on restart
- [ ] Batch processing with progress commits
- [ ] Full rebuild triggered on version mismatch
- [ ] Benchmark shows startup improvement

## Testing
- Criterion benchmarks with budgets and baseline comparison.
- Microbenchmarks for the specific optimization.
- CI regression guard with perf logs.

 {"id":"wa-3g9.5","title":"Performance benchmarks and budgets: Criterion suite with regression guards","description":"# Performance benchmarks and budgets (Criterion)

## Goal
Establish benchmarks for all performance-critical paths and prevent regressions with explicit, documented budgets.

This system sits on the hot path of “every command the user runs” (watch loop, pattern matching, policy checks). Performance regressions are user-visible.

## Benchmark suite (Criterion)
Benchmarks must target *real* hot paths and be runnable locally:

```rust
// benches/pattern_engine.rs
criterion_group!(
    pattern_benchmarks,
    bench_bloom_check,
    bench_aho_corasick_match,
    bench_full_pattern_pipeline,
);

// benches/ingest.rs
criterion_group!(
    ingest_benchmarks,
    bench_delta_extraction,
    bench_segment_persistence,
    bench_fts_indexing,
);
```

## What we benchmark (minimum set)
- Quick reject / prefilter:
  - `memchr`/bloom/no-match fast paths
  - worst-case “near match but reject” path
- Pattern pipeline:
  - no-hit text (must be very fast)
  - one-hit typical text
  - extraction path with captures
- Ingest/deltas:
  - delta extraction on typical scrollback
  - gap detection path
- Storage:
  - write queue throughput (bounded queue)
  - FTS indexing cost for typical segment sizes
- Query surfaces:
  - typical FTS query latency (scoped)
  - worst-case query guardrails (bounded)

## Performance budgets (absolute ceilings)
Budgets are set as “this must feel snappy to users” ceilings. Exact numbers may be tuned after first implementation, but budgets must exist before optimization work is called done.

| Operation | Budget (target ceiling) | Notes |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------|
| Bloom filter check | < 1µs | hot path prefilter |
| Pattern match (no hit) | < 10µs | must be cheap for most text |
| Pattern match (with hit) | < 100µs | includes extraction |
| Delta extraction | < 1ms | per pane per tick typical |
| FTS insert (single) | < 5ms | typical segment size |
| FTS query (typical) | < 50ms | scoped query; “feels instant” |
| Startup (no patterns) | < 100ms | CLI responsiveness |

## Regression detection strategy (CI realities)
Perf measurement is noisy on shared runners. The plan must be robust:

- Local dev loop:
  - `cargo bench` is the source of truth for tuning.
  - Provide a short “budget check” summary line per benchmark so developers can self-serve.

- CI loop (two-tier):
  1) **PR job (warn-only by default)**
     - Run benches and upload `criterion` output + a short summary artifact.
     - Fail only on catastrophic regressions (e.g., >2x slower OR exceeding a hard ceiling by a large margin).
  2) **Main/perf job (enforce budgets)**
     - Prefer a stable environment (self-hosted runner if available).
     - Enforce budgets strictly and record historical trend artifacts.

- Baselines:
  - Store a baseline artifact from main (or a tagged commit) and compare PR results against it.
  - Comparisons must ignore obviously-noisy fields and focus on timing deltas.

## Reporting
Example (desired UX):
```
$ cargo bench --bench pattern_engine

bloom_check         time:   [0.8 µs 0.9 µs 1.0 µs]  ✓ (budget: 1µs)
aho_corasick_match  time:   [8.2 µs 8.5 µs 8.8 µs]  ✓ (budget: 10µs)
full_pipeline       time:   [45 µs 48 µs 51 µs]     ✓ (budget: 100µs)
```

On regression, the report must include:
- which benchmark regressed
- old vs new timings
- which budget was exceeded (if any)
- where to find the artifacts (CI URL/path)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Criterion benchmarks exist for the minimum hot paths listed above.
- [ ] Budgets are documented and checked by an automated “budget check” step.
- [ ] CI produces artifacts for every run (raw criterion + summarized results).
- [ ] CI regression detection works (warn-only on PR; enforced on stable perf job).

## Testing
- Benchmark harness self-checks:
  - benches compile and run on supported platforms.
  - the budget-checking logic is covered by unit tests (parsing/threshold evaluation).
- CI regression guard tests:
  - include a small synthetic “known regression” fixture in test mode to verify:
    - the budget checker fails when it should
    - output includes the expected summary fields","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:35.705060335Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T19:54:37.164350673Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-perf","area-tests","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3g9.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3g9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-3g9.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3g9.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-3g9.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3g9.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-3g9.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3g9.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"wa-4df","title":"[Human command] `wa backup export` (database + config archive with checksum)","description":"# Task: wa backup export

## Goal
Create portable backup archives that users can store, copy, and restore from.

## Command Design
```bash
# Basic export
$ wa backup export
Backup saved: ~/.local/share/wa/backups/wa_backup_2026-01-18_143015.wa

# Custom output
$ wa backup export --output /path/to/backup.wa

# With compression
$ wa backup export --compress

# Include specific workspaces only
$ wa backup export --workspace /project/a --workspace /project/b

# Exclude large transcript data (config/events only)
$ wa backup export --metadata-only
```

## Output Format
Archive structure (tar + optional zstd):
```
backup.wa/
├── manifest.json      # Version, timestamp, checksums
├── schema_version     # Plain text: "3"
├── database.sql       # SQLite dump
├── config.toml        # Effective configuration
├── patterns/          # Custom pattern packs
│   └── custom.toml
└── checksums.sha256   # Per-file checksums
```

manifest.json:
```json
{
  "wa_version": "0.1.0",
  "schema_version": 3,
  "created_at": "2026-01-18T14:30:15Z",
  "workspaces": ["/project/a", "/project/b"],
  "stats": {
    "segments": 12345,
    "events": 567,
    "panes": 8
  },
  "checksum": "sha256:abc123..."
}
```

## Implementation Notes
- Use SQLite `.dump` for portability
- Acquire read lock during export (snapshot isolation)
- Stream large exports to avoid memory issues
- Verify checksum immediately after write

## Testing
- Unit tests: archive format validation, manifest generation
- Integration: export with active watcher, verify no corruption
- E2E: export + import round-trip preserves all data

## Acceptance Criteria
- Export creates valid, portable archive
- Checksum included and verified on write
- Progress shown for large databases
- Metadata-only mode for quick config backup

 {"id":"wa-4r7","title":"Multi-account exhaustion handling: all accounts at limit → graceful degradation","description":"# Multi-account exhaustion handling

## Purpose
Handle the scenario where ALL configured accounts have reached their usage limits simultaneously.

## Problem
Current usage limit workflow assumes at least one account is available for failover.
When all accounts are exhausted, we need graceful degradation:
1. Don't attempt impossible failovers
2. Provide clear status to user
3. Schedule auto-resume at earliest reset time
4. Support notification of this critical state

## Detection
```rust
// In account selection logic
let available = accounts.iter()
    .filter(|a| a.percent_remaining > 0.0 || a.reset_time < now)
    .collect::<Vec<_>>();

if available.is_empty() {
    return Err(AccountExhausted {
        next_reset: accounts.iter()
            .map(|a| a.reset_time)
            .min(),
        accounts_checked: accounts.len(),
    });
}
```

## Workflow Behavior
When `AccountExhausted` detected:

### Step 1: Capture state
- Save current session transcript
- Record in-progress task (if determinable)
- Log which accounts were checked

### Step 2: Calculate resume time
```rust
let earliest_reset = accounts.iter()
    .filter_map(|a| a.reset_time)
    .min();
```

### Step 3: Persist "awaiting reset" state
```sql
INSERT INTO workflow_paused 
(pane_id, reason, resume_at, state_snapshot)
VALUES (?, 'all_accounts_exhausted', ?, ?);
```

### Step 4: Notify user
```
🔴 All accounts exhausted

All 3 configured OpenAI accounts have reached their usage limits.

Next reset: account-2 @ 2026-01-19 00:00 UTC (6h 23m)

Options:
  1. Wait for reset (wa will auto-resume if enabled)
  2. Add new account: wa accounts add
  3. Continue manually in exhausted account (with limits)

Status: wa accounts status
Resume: wa workflow resume --when-available
```

### Step 5: Schedule resume (optional)
If auto-resume enabled:
- Create timer/cron for earliest reset time
- Resume workflow when triggered

## Configuration
```toml
[accounts.exhaustion]
notify_channels = ["desktop", "webhook"]
auto_resume_on_reset = true
save_transcript = true
```

## Robot Mode Error
```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "error": {
    "code": "E_ALL_ACCOUNTS_EXHAUSTED",
    "message": "All configured accounts have reached usage limits",
    "data": {
      "accounts_checked": 3,
      "next_reset": "2026-01-19T00:00:00Z",
      "next_reset_account": "account-2"
    }
  }
}
```

## Testing
- Unit: Account selection returns exhaustion error correctly
- Unit: Resume time calculation
- Integration: Workflow pauses correctly
- E2E: Full exhaustion → notification → resume flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Exhaustion detected when all accounts at limit
- [ ] Clear user notification with reset times
- [ ] State preserved for resume
- [ ] Auto-resume works when enabled
- [ ] Robot mode returns structured error","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:14:14.002558283Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T20:41:46.028393618Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-accounts","area-workflows","phase-2"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:59Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-4ur","title":"E2E CI integration: GitHub Actions workflow with artifact collection","description":"# E2E CI Integration: GitHub Actions workflow with artifact collection

## Purpose
Run E2E tests in CI with detailed logging and artifact preservation.

## Implementation

### GitHub Actions Workflow
```yaml
# .github/workflows/e2e.yml
name: E2E Tests

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always
  E2E_LOG_LEVEL: debug
  E2E_TIMEOUT: 300

jobs:
  e2e-tests:
    name: E2E Test Suite
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30

    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Rust
        uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable

      - name: Cache cargo
        uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@v2

      - name: Install WezTerm
        run: |
          curl -fsSL https://apt.fury.io/wez/gpg.key | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/wezterm-fury.gpg
          echo 'deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/wezterm-fury.gpg] https://apt.fury.io/wez/ * *' | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/wezterm.list
          sudo apt update
          sudo apt install -y wezterm

      - name: Build wa (release)
        run: cargo build --release

      - name: Create logs directory
        run: mkdir -p tests/e2e/logs

      - name: Run E2E tests
        run: |
          echo "Starting E2E test suite..."
          cargo test --test e2e -- --test-threads=1 --nocapture 2>&1 | tee tests/e2e/logs/test_output.log
        env:
          RUST_BACKTRACE: 1
          WA_BIN: target/release/wa

      - name: Upload test logs
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: e2e-logs-${{ github.run_id }}
          path: |
            tests/e2e/logs/
            target/release/wa
          retention-days: 7

      - name: Upload coverage (if available)
        if: success()
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          files: ./coverage.json
          fail_ci_if_error: false
```

### Test Runner Script
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# tests/e2e/scripts/run_all.sh
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../../.." && pwd)"
LOG_DIR="$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/e2e/logs"

# Colors
RED='\033[0;31m'
GREEN='\033[0;32m'
YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
NC='\033[0m'

log() { echo -e "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] $*"; }
log_ok() { log "${GREEN}✓${NC} $*"; }
log_err() { log "${RED}✗${NC} $*"; }
log_warn() { log "${YELLOW}!${NC} $*"; }

# Setup
mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
MAIN_LOG="$LOG_DIR/e2e_run_$TIMESTAMP.log"

log "E2E Test Suite starting..."
log "Logs will be written to: $LOG_DIR"

# Pre-flight checks
log "Running pre-flight checks..."

if ! command -v wezterm &> /dev/null; then
    log_err "WezTerm not found. Install it first."
    exit 1
fi
log_ok "WezTerm found: $(wezterm --version)"

if [[ ! -f "$PROJECT_ROOT/target/release/wa" ]]; then
    log_warn "wa binary not found. Building..."
    cargo build --release
fi
log_ok "wa binary found"

# Run tests
log "Running E2E tests..."
FAILED=0
PASSED=0

for test_file in "$PROJECT_ROOT"/tests/e2e/scenarios/*.rs; do
    test_name=$(basename "$test_file" .rs)
    log "Running: $test_name"

    if cargo test --test "e2e_$test_name" -- --nocapture >> "$MAIN_LOG" 2>&1; then
        log_ok "$test_name passed"
        ((PASSED++))
    else
        log_err "$test_name failed (see $MAIN_LOG)"
        ((FAILED++))
    fi
done

# Summary
log ""
log "========================================="
log "E2E Test Summary"
log "========================================="
log "Passed: $PASSED"
log "Failed: $FAILED"
log "Logs: $LOG_DIR"
log "========================================="

if [[ $FAILED -gt 0 ]]; then
    log_err "Some tests failed!"
    exit 1
fi

log_ok "All E2E tests passed!"
```

### Log Aggregation
```bash
# tests/e2e/scripts/aggregate_logs.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Combine all test logs into a single report

LOG_DIR="${1:-tests/e2e/logs}"
OUTPUT="$LOG_DIR/combined_report.md"

echo "# E2E Test Report" > "$OUTPUT"
echo "Generated: $(date)" >> "$OUTPUT"
echo "" >> "$OUTPUT"

for log_file in "$LOG_DIR"/*.log; do
    test_name=$(basename "$log_file" .log)
    echo "## $test_name" >> "$OUTPUT"
    echo '```' >> "$OUTPUT"
    tail -100 "$log_file" >> "$OUTPUT"
    echo '```' >> "$OUTPUT"
    echo "" >> "$OUTPUT"
done

echo "Report generated: $OUTPUT"
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] GitHub Actions workflow runs on PR and push
- [ ] WezTerm installed in CI environment
- [ ] All test logs uploaded as artifacts
- [ ] Test failures clearly reported
- [ ] Run time < 15 minutes

## Testing
- CI self-check: workflow runs the E2E suite and produces artifacts on both success and failure.
- Artifact validation: assert that log files and the main summary report are uploaded.
- Time budget: CI job enforces max runtime and fails with a clear message if exceeded.

 {"id":"wa-4vx","title":"[EPIC] Phase 1: Foundation - Core Infrastructure","description":"# Phase 1: Foundation - Core Infrastructure

## Overview
This epic covers the foundational infrastructure for WezTerm Automata (wa), establishing the core capabilities needed before any higher-level features can be built. This is the critical path - nothing else can proceed until these components exist.

## Strategic Importance
The foundation phase establishes the architectural patterns and core abstractions that ALL other code will depend on. Mistakes here propagate everywhere. We must:
- Get the WezTerm client abstraction right (it's the I/O layer for everything)
- Design storage schema carefully (migrations are painful)
- Build the pattern engine for extensibility (we'll add many patterns over time)
- Create the watcher with proper delta extraction (performance-critical hot path)

## Key Components

### 1. Cargo Workspace Setup
- wa (main binary)
- wa-core (library crate)
- wa-browser (browser automation, optional feature)
- wa-mcp (MCP server, optional feature)

### 2. WezTerm CLI Client Wrapper
- Thin wrapper around \`wezterm cli\` commands
- list-clients, list-panes (ListPaneResponse parsing)
- get-text (GetTextResponse with coordinates)
- send-text (with or without paste-mode)
- spawn (create panes in specific domains)
- Abstraction layer for future vendoring

### 3. SQLite Storage with FTS5
- Schema: domains, panes, captures, captures_fts, events, agent_sessions, workflow_runs, accounts, config
- Delta-first storage (append chunks, not full pane content)
- Gap detection and tracking
- FTS5 for full-text search of all captured output

### 4. Basic Pattern Engine
- Aho-Corasick for literal patterns (O(n) multi-pattern matching)
- Quick reject with memchr (eliminate 99%+ of text before regex)
- Pattern packs: core.codex, core.claude_code, core.gemini
- Extraction via named capture groups

### 5. Watcher Daemon
- Adaptive polling (faster when active, slower when idle)
- Delta extraction algorithm (overlap matching for reliable diff)
- Event emission on pattern detection
- Per-pane state tracking

### 6. Basic Robot Mode
- wa robot state - Get all panes as JSON
- wa robot get-text <pane_id> - Read pane output
- wa robot send <pane_id> "<text>" - Send input to pane
- JSON envelope with ok/data/error/hint/elapsed_ms

## Success Criteria
- [ ] \`wa watch\` runs and captures pane output continuously
- [ ] \`wa robot state\` returns JSON list of all panes
- [ ] \`wa robot get-text <id>\` returns pane content
- [ ] \`wa robot send <id> "test"\` sends text to pane
- [ ] FTS search finds text in captured output
- [ ] Pattern engine detects compaction, usage limits for at least one agent
- [ ] Tests pass, clippy clean, formatted

## Technical Decisions

### Why SQLite?
- Zero-config deployment
- Excellent FTS5 for search
- WAL mode for concurrent access
- Single-file portability

### Why Aho-Corasick + Regex?
- Aho-Corasick: O(n) for matching multiple patterns simultaneously
- Quick Reject: Eliminates 99%+ of text before regex
- Regex for extraction: Named captures for structured data

### Why adaptive polling vs push?
- WezTerm CLI doesn't support push notifications
- Adaptive polling balances responsiveness vs CPU
- Future vendoring may enable true event subscription

## Dependencies
- This epic has NO dependencies (it's the foundation)
- ALL other epics depend on this one

## Estimated Scope
- Foundation for everything else
- Must be rock-solid before proceeding

## Testing
This phase is the “spine” of the whole system, so tests here must be **fast, deterministic, and multi-layer**:

- Unit tests (tight, deterministic):
  - WezTerm CLI JSON parsing fixtures and edge cases (see `wa-4vx.2.5`, `wa-4vx.10.4`).
  - Storage schema/query helpers (including audit/redaction invariants) (see `wa-4vx.3.9`).
  - Pattern detection core: quick reject, extraction, pack semantics, and stable rule IDs (see `wa-4vx.5.*`).
  - Safety/policy decisions and redaction behavior (see `wa-4vx.8.*`).
  - Robot mode JSON envelope + stable error codes (see `wa-4vx.7.8`, `wa-4vx.7.10`).

- Integration tests (no real WezTerm required):
  - Synthetic/fixture delta streams drive watcher/ingest → storage → search → robot read paths (see `wa-4vx.6.4`).
  - Explicit negative-path tests for gaps/backpressure and “unhandled event” flows.

- E2E test scripts (real-ish wiring, with artifacts):
  - Must follow the E2E harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`) and use structured logging (`wa-4vx.6.5`).
  - Cover core wiring and reliability scenarios (examples: `wa-4vx.10.14`, `wa-4vx.10.15`, `wa-4vx.10.19`, `wa-4vx.10.21`, `wa-4vx.10.22`).

- Artifact requirements (for every integration/E2E run):
  - Structured logs (machine-parseable), a per-case metadata file, and failure screenshots when available.
  - A redacted audit extract verifying “deny” decisions never leak secrets.
  - A deterministic DB snapshot/export sufficient to reproduce assertions offline.

- Performance regression gates:
  - Criterion budgets for the hot path (delta extraction, pattern scan, FTS queries) live in `wa-4vx.10.2`.
  - When budgets fail, output must include enough profiling/log context to localize the regression.

Note: this phase is linked under the `wa-nu4` umbrella milestone for tracking, but it should be treated as the root prerequisite for later phases.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-w5l","title":"[Human command] `wa config export/import` for config-only migration and sharing","description":"# Task: wa config export/import

## Goal
Allow users to share configurations or migrate config between machines without data.

## Command Design
```bash
# Export current effective config
$ wa config export
# Saved to: ~/.config/wa/exports/wa_config_2026-01-18.toml

$ wa config export --output my-setup.toml

# Import and merge config
$ wa config import my-setup.toml
Importing configuration...
  [patterns] 3 new rules
  [notifications] webhook config updated
  [safety] policy unchanged

$ wa config import shared-team-config.toml --dry-run
Would update:
  [patterns.custom_rules] +3 rules
  [notifications.webhook.url] http://... → http://...new
  [safety.policy] no change

# Replace entirely
$ wa config import my-setup.toml --replace
⚠️  This will replace your entire configuration!
Backup saved to: ~/.config/wa/exports/backup_pre_import.toml
Continue? [y/N]
```

## Export Contents
```toml
# wa configuration export
# Exported: 2026-01-18T14:30:15Z
# wa version: 0.1.0

[patterns]
# Custom patterns defined by user
[[patterns.custom_rules]]
id = "my-error-pattern"
pattern = "FATAL:"
# ...

[notifications]
# User notification settings
# ...

[safety]
# Policy configuration
# ...

# Note: Secrets are NOT exported
# Webhook URLs with tokens are redacted: http://...?token=[REDACTED]
```

## Safety
- Never export secrets (API keys, tokens in URLs)
- Require confirmation for --replace
- Create backup before destructive import
- Validate config syntax before import

## Testing
- Unit tests: export format, merge logic, redaction
- Integration: export/import round-trip
- E2E: share config between two wa instances

## Acceptance Criteria
- Export creates valid, importable TOML
- Secrets are redacted in exports
- Import shows diff before applying
- Merge mode preserves user customizations

 {"id":"wa-w8s","title":"E2E test framework infrastructure: harness, logging macros, custom assertions","description":"# E2E Test Framework Infrastructure

## Purpose
Build the core test harness that all E2E tests will use.

## Implementation

### Test Harness Library
```rust
// tests/e2e/harness/mod.rs
pub struct E2ETestHarness {
    wa_process: Option<Child>,
    test_db: TempDir,
    log_file: File,
    start_time: Instant,
}

impl E2ETestHarness {
    pub async fn new(test_name: &str) -> Result<Self> {
        let test_db = TempDir::new()?;
        let log_file = create_log_file(test_name)?;

        log_info!(&log_file, "=== Starting E2E Test: {} ===", test_name);

        Ok(Self {
            wa_process: None,
            test_db,
            log_file,
            start_time: Instant::now(),
        })
    }

    pub async fn start_wa(&mut self, args: &[&str]) -> Result<()> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Starting wa with args: {:?}", args);

        let process = Command::new("target/release/wa")
            .args(args)
            .env("WA_DB_PATH", self.test_db.path())
            .env("WA_LOG_LEVEL", "debug")
            .spawn()?;

        self.wa_process = Some(process);

        // Wait for ready
        self.wait_for_ready().await?;
        log_info!(&self.log_file, "wa started successfully");
        Ok(())
    }

    pub async fn inject_output(&self, pane_id: u32, text: &str) -> Result<()> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Injecting output to pane {}: {:?}", pane_id, text);
        // Mock or real injection
    }

    pub async fn wait_for_event(&self, rule_id: &str, timeout: Duration) -> Result<Event> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Waiting for event: {} (timeout: {:?})", rule_id, timeout);
        // Poll for event
    }

    pub async fn assert_db_contains(&self, query: &str) -> Result<()> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Checking DB for: {}", query);
        // Query and assert
    }
}

impl Drop for E2ETestHarness {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if let Some(mut process) = self.wa_process.take() {
            let _ = process.kill();
        }
        let elapsed = self.start_time.elapsed();
        log_info!(&self.log_file, "=== E2E Test completed ({:.2}s) ===", elapsed.as_secs_f64());
    }
}
```

### Detailed Logging
```rust
// tests/e2e/harness/logging.rs
use std::fs::File;
use std::io::Write;
use chrono::Utc;

pub fn create_log_file(test_name: &str) -> Result<File> {
    let timestamp = Utc::now().format("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S");
    let path = format!("tests/e2e/logs/{}_{}.log", test_name, timestamp);
    std::fs::create_dir_all("tests/e2e/logs")?;
    File::create(path)
}

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! log_info {
    ($file:expr, $($arg:tt)*) => {{
        let msg = format!($($arg)*);
        let timestamp = chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%.3fZ");
        let line = format!("[{}] [INFO] {}\n", timestamp, msg);
        let _ = $file.write_all(line.as_bytes());
        eprintln!("{}", line.trim());
    }};
}

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! log_debug {
    ($file:expr, $($arg:tt)*) => {{
        let msg = format!($($arg)*);
        let timestamp = chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%.3fZ");
        let line = format!("[{}] [DEBUG] {}\n", timestamp, msg);
        let _ = $file.write_all(line.as_bytes());
        if std::env::var("E2E_LOG_LEVEL").map(|l| l == "debug").unwrap_or(false) {
            eprintln!("{}", line.trim());
        }
    }};
}

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! log_error {
    ($file:expr, $($arg:tt)*) => {{
        let msg = format!($($arg)*);
        let timestamp = chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%.3fZ");
        let line = format!("[{}] [ERROR] {}\n", timestamp, msg);
        let _ = $file.write_all(line.as_bytes());
        eprintln!("\x1b[31m{}\x1b[0m", line.trim());
    }};
}
```

### Custom Assertions
```rust
// tests/e2e/harness/assertions.rs
use crate::harness::E2ETestHarness;

pub async fn assert_event_detected(
    harness: &E2ETestHarness,
    rule_id: &str,
    timeout: Duration,
) -> Result<Event> {
    let event = harness.wait_for_event(rule_id, timeout).await
        .with_context(|| format!("Expected event {} not detected within {:?}", rule_id, timeout))?;

    log_info!(&harness.log_file, "✓ Event detected: {}", rule_id);
    Ok(event)
}

pub async fn assert_workflow_completed(
    harness: &E2ETestHarness,
    workflow_id: &str,
    timeout: Duration,
) -> Result<WorkflowResult> {
    // Poll workflow status until complete or timeout
}

pub async fn assert_policy_denied(
    harness: &E2ETestHarness,
    action: &str,
) -> Result<PolicyDenial> {
    // Attempt action and verify denial
}
```

## Testing
- Meta-tests: Harness correctly starts/stops wa
- Logging tests: Logs written correctly
- Assertion tests: Assertions work as expected

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Harness can start/stop wa process
- [ ] Detailed logs written for every operation
- [ ] Custom assertions provide clear error messages
- [ ] Cleanup works even on test failure

-{"id":"wa-wb6","title":"[Human command] `wa backup import` (restore with verification and safety checks)","description":"# Task: wa backup import

## Goal
Safely restore data from backup archives with verification and user confirmation.

## Command Design
```bash
# Verify backup without importing
$ wa backup import backup.wa --verify
Backup verified:
  Version: 0.1.0 (compatible)
  Created: 2026-01-18T14:30:15Z
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Checksum: OK

# Dry-run to see what would change
$ wa backup import backup.wa --dry-run
Would restore:
  + 12,345 segments (current: 8,000)
  + 567 events (current: 300)
  Config: 3 differences

# Full restore (replaces current)
$ wa backup import backup.wa --replace
⚠️  This will replace ALL current data!
Current data will be backed up to: ~/.local/share/wa/backups/pre_import_2026-01-18.wa
Continue? [y/N]

# Merge mode (keep newer, import older)
$ wa backup import backup.wa --merge
Merging...
  Imported: 4,345 segments
  Skipped: 8,000 (newer local)
  Conflicts: 0
```

## Safety Requirements
1. **Automatic pre-import backup**: Before any destructive import, create backup of current state
2. **Checksum verification**: Reject imports with invalid checksums
3. **Schema compatibility**: Check version and migrate if needed
4. **Explicit confirmation**: Require --yes or interactive confirmation for destructive ops
5. **Watcher coordination**: Require watcher to be stopped, or use --force with warning

## Error Handling
```bash
$ wa backup import corrupted.wa
Error: Backup verification failed
  Expected checksum: abc123
  Actual checksum: def456
  
Backup may be corrupted. If you trust the source:
  wa backup import corrupted.wa --skip-verify --force
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: verification logic, merge algorithm
- Integration: import into empty DB, import with conflicts
- E2E: full restore cycle, error on corrupt backup

## Acceptance Criteria
- Import verifies checksum before any changes
- Pre-import backup created automatically
- Dry-run mode shows exactly what would change
- Merge mode preserves newer local data
- Clear errors for corrupt/incompatible backups

 {"id":"wa-y2e","title":"Robot mode dry-run: wa robot … --dry-run previews (send/workflow/events)","description":"# Robot mode dry-run previews (`wa robot … --dry-run`)

## Goal
Extend dry-run preview semantics to robot-mode commands so an AI agent can plan safely before taking actions.

This task is specifically about **robot mode** (machine output), and complements the **human** dry-run work in `wa-1pe.*`.

## Scope
Add `--dry-run` to robot commands that would otherwise mutate state:
- `wa robot send --dry-run …`
- `wa robot workflow … --dry-run`

Also add “planning previews” for read-only commands that *cause the agent to act*:
- `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run` (preview which workflows would run, without running them)

Non-goals (for v0.1):
- actually granting approvals from robot mode (approval is intentionally a human/interactive surface)

## Output contract
- Uses the standard robot envelope (`wa-4vx.7.1`, schemas in `wa-4vx.7.10`).
- Must be deterministic and redacted.
- Must never:
  - send text
  - acquire locks/reservations
  - mutate DB state (other than unavoidable read-side effects like opening the DB)

## Examples

### `wa robot send --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "send",
    "dry_run": true,
    "target": {
      "pane_id": 3,
      "title": "codex @ /project",
      "agent_type": "codex"
    },
    "input": {
      "text_preview": "continue",
      "text_len": 8
    },
    "policy_evaluation": {
      "decision": "allow",
      "capabilities": {
        "prompt_active": true,
        "command_running": false,
        "alt_screen": false,
        "recent_gap": false
      },
      "command_safety": {
        "is_command_candidate": false,
        "decision": "allow"
      }
    },
    "would_execute": true
  },
  "error": null
}
```

### `wa robot workflow run --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "workflow_run",
    "dry_run": true,
    "workflow": "handle_compaction",
    "target": {"pane_id": 3, "title": "claude @ /project"},
    "steps_preview": [
      {"step": 1, "name": "verify_compaction_state", "kind": "check_pattern"},
      {"step": 2, "name": "inject_context", "kind": "send_text", "text_preview": "Please re-read …"},
      {"step": 3, "name": "wait_ack", "kind": "wait_for", "pattern": "I've reviewed"}
    ],
    "estimated_duration_ms": 30000
  },
  "error": null
}
```

### `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "events",
    "dry_run": true,
    "events": [
      {
        "event_id": 123,
        "rule_id": "codex.usage_limit_reached",
        "pane_id": 9,
        "would_handle_with": {
          "workflow": "handle_usage_limits",
          "first_step": "exit_codex",
          "estimated_duration_ms": 60000
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  "error": null
}
```

## Design notes
- Robot dry-run should reuse the same planning logic as human dry-run where possible:
  - shared `DryRunReport` structs (different renderers)
  - same PolicyEngine evaluation paths
- If `--dry-run` is combined with `--wait-for` style arguments, the preview should include:
  - what matcher would be used
  - timeout/backoff parameters
  - but must not actually wait

## Testing
- Unit/contract tests:
  - `--dry-run` never calls SendText / workflow execution
  - outputs validate against JSON schemas (`wa-4vx.7.10`)
  - redaction invariants hold (no secrets in previews)

- E2E:
  - add an adapter case to the E2E registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`) that runs:
    - `wa robot send --dry-run …`
    - `wa robot workflow … --dry-run`
    - `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run`
  - assert:
    - outputs are parseable JSON
    - `dry_run: true`
    - no audit “action executed” rows were created

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `wa robot send --dry-run` returns a deterministic, schema-valid preview including policy evaluation.
- [ ] `wa robot workflow … --dry-run` returns a deterministic, schema-valid step preview.
- [ ] `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run` previews which workflows would run.
- [ ] Dry-run paths do not acquire locks or mutate panes/DB state.

 {"id":"wa-y59","title":"Secure file permissions: restrictive umask for DB, logs, sockets","description":"# Task: Secure File Permissions

## Goal
Ensure wa files are created with appropriate permissions to prevent unauthorized access.

## Files Requiring Protection
1. **Database (wa.db)**: Contains terminal transcripts - may include secrets typed by user
2. **Logs**: May contain debugging info that includes sensitive paths/commands
3. **IPC sockets**: Used for inter-process communication
4. **Config files**: May contain webhook URLs with tokens
5. **Backup files**: Contains all above

## Implementation

### Permission Requirements
| File Type | Unix Mode | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Database | 0600 | Owner read/write only |
| Logs | 0600 | Owner read/write only |
| IPC socket | 0600 | Owner only |
| Lock files | 0644 | Others can check existence |
| Config | 0600 | May contain secrets |
| .wa directory | 0700 | Owner traverse only |

### Implementation
```rust
use std::os::unix::fs::PermissionsExt;

pub fn create_secure_file(path: &Path) -> io::Result<File> {
    let file = File::create(path)?;
    let mut perms = file.metadata()?.permissions();
    perms.set_mode(0o600);
    file.set_permissions(perms)?;
    Ok(file)
}

pub fn create_secure_directory(path: &Path) -> io::Result<()> {
    fs::create_dir_all(path)?;
    let mut perms = fs::metadata(path)?.permissions();
    perms.set_mode(0o700);
    fs::set_permissions(path, perms)?;
    Ok(())
}
```

### Startup Validation
On startup, check existing file permissions and warn if too permissive:
```
WARNING: Database permissions too open (0644)
  File: /home/user/.wa/wa.db
  Recommended: chmod 600 /home/user/.wa/wa.db
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: file creation with correct permissions
- Integration: wa doctor checks permissions
- E2E: new installation creates secure files

## Acceptance Criteria
- All sensitive files created with restrictive permissions
- wa doctor warns about insecure permissions
- Documentation explains security model","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:59:49.464206636Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:47.045518770Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:47.045439511Z","close_reason":"Implementation verified complete: Secure file permissions throughout codebase. storage.rs:977-1001 - set_permissions() creates DB/WAL/SHM files with 0o600, parent dirs with 0o700. logging.rs:119-161 - set_file_permissions() for log files at 0o600, log dirs at 0o700. config.rs:1662-1692 - check_permission() in wa doctor validates all sensitive paths (workspace dir, logs dir, crash dir, db, log files, ipc socket, config). Tests at storage.rs:5449-5459 and config.rs:2377-2402 verify permissions. main.rs:3106 outputs chmod recommendations for insecure files.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-security","area-storage","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-y59","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:12Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-y59","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:12Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-y59","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.9.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:12Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-y6g","title":"Schema migration framework: versioned migrations with rollback support","description":"# Task: Schema Migration Framework

## Goal
Enable wa to safely upgrade its database schema across versions without data loss.

## Why This Matters
As wa evolves:
- New columns/tables needed for features
- Indexes may change for performance
- Data format may evolve

Without migrations, users would need to:
- Delete database and lose history
- Manually transform data

## Design

### Migration Files
```
src/storage/migrations/
├── mod.rs
├── v001_initial_schema.rs
├── v002_add_event_metadata.rs
├── v003_fts_improvements.rs
└── v004_workflow_state.rs
```

### Migration Structure
```rust
pub struct Migration {
    pub version: u32,
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub up: fn(&Connection) -> Result<()>,
    pub down: Option<fn(&Connection) -> Result<()>>,
}

// Example migration
pub static V002_ADD_EVENT_METADATA: Migration = Migration {
    version: 2,
    name: "add_event_metadata",
    up: |conn| {
        conn.execute_batch("
            ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN metadata TEXT;
            CREATE INDEX idx_events_metadata ON events(json_extract(metadata, '$.type'));
        ")?;
        Ok(())
    },
    down: Some(|conn| {
        conn.execute_batch("
            DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_events_metadata;
            ALTER TABLE events DROP COLUMN metadata;
        ")?;
        Ok(())
    }),
};
```

### Migration Runner
```rust
pub struct Migrator {
    migrations: Vec<&'static Migration>,
}

impl Migrator {
    pub fn run(&self, conn: &Connection) -> Result<MigrationReport> {
        let current = self.get_current_version(conn)?;
        let target = self.latest_version();
        
        tracing::info\!(
            current_version = current,
            target_version = target,
            "Starting migration"
        );
        
        if current == target {
            return Ok(MigrationReport::AlreadyCurrent);
        }
        
        // Run migrations in transaction
        conn.execute("BEGIN EXCLUSIVE", [])?;
        
        for migration in &self.migrations {
            if migration.version > current {
                tracing::info\!(
                    version = migration.version,
                    name = migration.name,
                    "Running migration"
                );
                
                (migration.up)(conn)?;
                self.record_migration(conn, migration)?;
            }
        }
        
        conn.execute("COMMIT", [])?;
        
        Ok(MigrationReport::Migrated {
            from: current,
            to: target,
        })
    }
}
```

### CLI Integration
```bash
# Automatic on startup (default)
$ wa watch
Migrating database: v1 → v3
  Running: v002_add_event_metadata... OK
  Running: v003_fts_improvements... OK
Migration complete.

# Manual migration
$ wa db migrate
Current version: 1
Target version: 3
Run migrations? [y/N]

# Check status without migrating
$ wa db migrate --status
Current version: 1
Available migrations:
  ✓ v001_initial_schema (applied)
  ○ v002_add_event_metadata (pending)
  ○ v003_fts_improvements (pending)
```

## Safety
- All migrations run in transaction
- Backup created before migration (if large changes)
- Down migrations available for rollback
- Version tracked in dedicated table

## Testing
- Unit tests: each migration up/down
- Integration: migrate from v1 to vN
- E2E: upgrade simulation with real data

## Acceptance Criteria
- Migrations run automatically on startup
- Failed migration rolls back cleanly
- Migration status visible via CLI
- Down migrations available for emergencies
